_note: Launch-day X thread for @przm_ai. Edit before --send. Each entry must be ≤280 chars (the script validates). Posts chain as replies in order.
tweets:
I built a benchmark for something nobody is measuring: how multi-agent AI systems collapse to confidently-wrong answers when one agent gets seeded with a fake-but-confident position.

Same model, different framework: 7.3× difference in collapse rate.

przm.sh

thread ↓

The setup is simple. N agents debate a question. One is given a wrong answer plus a polished rationale. The rest start correct.

We measure how many of the correct ones end up agreeing with the wrong one. And how much of that depends on the framework, not the model.

Headline number is the same-model, different-framework run.

gpt-4o-mini, 30 scenarios, 3 agents × 3 rounds:
• Hand-rolled synchronous rounds: 96.7% collapse
• Microsoft AutoGen RoundRobin: 13.3% collapse

7.3× difference from orchestration alone. Model held constant.

The surprise: AutoGen's RoundRobin (where every agent sees peers' same-round messages) actually resists convergence harder than the hand-rolled baseline.

Correct agents reinforce each other before the confederate's confidence has time to compound. I had predicted the opposite.

Methodology is deterministic. No LLM in the grading loop.

Every result is an Ed25519-signed receipt: adapter version, LLM, fixture SHA, per-round transcripts. Verify in your browser at /verify.

Open source. If the method is wrong, you find out before I can hide it.

przm.sh
